paper.title: Hematogenous neuroinvasion and genotype-dependent transmission of influenza A H5N1 viruses in the cat host

review.summary: This study characterizes the pathogenesis and transmissibility of two H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b genotypes (B3.13 and D1.1) in domestic cats. The key finding is that H5N1 achieves neuroinvasion via a hematogenous route — the virus infects brain microvascular endothelial cells to cross the blood-brain barrier, then spreads to astrocytes and neurons causing multifocal encephalitis. Strikingly, the two genotypes show dramatically different pathogenesis and transmission profiles: B3.13 causes rapid-onset disease with high shedding and efficient cat-to-cat transmission (75%), while D1.1 causes protracted disease with lower shedding and no detectable transmission.

key_molecular_findings.mechanism: Virus infects endothelial cells of the blood-brain barrier as the primary route of CNS entry (hematogenous neuroinvasion rather than olfactory nerve route)
After crossing the BBB, virus spreads from endothelial cells to adjacent astrocytes
Virus spreads from astrocytes/endothelial cells to neurons, causing multifocal encephalitis
Primary replication site; B3.13 genotype replicates more efficiently leading to higher shedding titers

review.experimental_approach: In vivo cat infection model comparing two H5N1 genotypes (B3.13 and D1.1). Likely includes viral titration from multiple organs, histopathology with immunohistochemistry to identify infected cell types in the brain, and direct-contact transmission experiments.
review.evidence_quality: strong — in vivo mammalian model with direct relevance to zoonotic risk, systematic comparison of two circulating genotypes, identification of cellular route of neuroinvasion
review.hpmi_relevance: Demonstrates the cellular route by which H5N1 achieves neuroinvasion in a mammalian host (hematogenous, not olfactory). Establishes that genotype-specific differences in the virus (B3.13 vs D1.1 reassortant constellations) dramatically affect pathogenesis and transmission outcomes in mammals. However, the study appears to focus on cellular-level host-pathogen interactions rather than specific protein-protein molecular interactions.
review.recommendation_reason: Important for understanding H5N1 pathogenesis in mammals and zoonotic risk, especially given the current epizootic. The hematogenous BBB-crossing mechanism and genotype-dependent transmission are significant findings. However, rated 'worth_discussing' rather than 'must_read' for HPMI because it appears to characterize cellular tropism and in vivo pathology rather than identifying specific molecular interactions (protein-protein, gene regulation) that are the primary focus of our triage.
review.review_note: Full text access was blocked by egress proxy. Review based on abstract and secondary source summaries from web search. Specific molecular mechanisms (e.g., receptor usage, immune evasion) may be described in the full paper but could not be assessed.
